Api Development

Web Scraping

Artificial Intelligence

Software Development

Twitter

7 min read
Just now

--

X removed the expensive entry ticket, introduced pay-per-use pricing, and even added support for AI agents. It looks like the developer platform is finally back. The numbers tell a more complicated story.
A few months ago, I started looking seriously at adding Twitter data to a product I was building.
The timing seemed perfect.
X had replaced its old fixed subscription tiers with pay-per-use pricing. Developers could create an app, purchase credits, and start making requests without committing hundreds or thousands of dollars every month.
There were official SDKs, better documentation, and even an MCP server for developers using tools such as Cursor and Windsurf.
After years of pushing developers away, X appeared to be inviting them back.
I expected the official API to be the obvious choice.
Then I started modelling what a real monitoring product would actually need: continuous searches, thousands of new posts, complete reply threads, multiple customers, and AI agents that explore far more than a human normally would.
The first request was easy.
The twentieth page of replies was not.
Neither was the bill.
The API was open again, but access was no longer the main problem. The real questions were whether the economics could scale, whether the limits matched modern applications, and whether developers trusted X enough to build on it again.
That investigation is ultimately why we added Twitter to FetchLayer.
But before getting into what we built, it is worth looking at why half a cent per post becomes expensive much faster than it sounds.
Half a Cent Adds Up Fast
X currently charges $0.005 for every post returned by the API.
Retrieve 100 posts and you pay around $0.50. For a small script, personal dashboard, or weekend project, that may be completely reasonable.
But monitoring products do not retrieve 100 posts once.
They search continuously.
Imagine tracking 100 keywords and retrieving 100 new posts for each one every day:

• 10,000 post reads per day

• 300,000 post reads per month

• $1,500 per month for post reads alone
At one million post reads, the cost reaches $5,000.
At two million, it reaches $10,000 — and two million post reads is also the monthly cap for self-serve access. Beyond that, you need an Enterprise agreement.
X generally avoids charging twice for the same resource within a 24-hour window. That helps with caching, but it does not solve the cost of discovering new content.
And discovery is the expensive part.
Suppose you are building a lead-monitoring tool. To find ten people complaining about their current payment provider, your system may need to retrieve and evaluate thousands of posts.
The customer only cares about the ten useful results.
Your application pays to inspect everything required to find them.
The expensive part is not showing the ten posts that matter. It is reading the other 9,990 posts required to discover them.
The same problem affects brand monitoring, competitor research, sentiment analysis, trend detection, academic research, and AI agents.
Pay-per-use removes the expensive entry ticket.
It does not necessarily make the API affordable when your product grows.
A Conversation Is Not One API Request
The limitations become even clearer when you try to retrieve the replies under a post.
There is no single request that returns a complete, nested discussion ready for your application to use.
Instead, replies share a conversation_id. You search for them using a query like:
conversation_id:1891234567890123456X returns the posts belonging to that conversation. Your application must then determine which reply belongs to which parent and reconstruct the discussion itself.
Recent search returns a maximum of 100 posts per request. A conversation containing 2,000 replies therefore requires at least 20 paginated requests.
After that, you still need to:

• Identify each reply’s parent

• Rebuild the nested structure

• Handle missing or deleted posts

• Order the branches correctly

• Retry failed or rate-limited pages
Every reply is also an individual billable post read.
With X, the unit you want is a conversation. The unit you pay for is every individual post needed to reconstruct it.
X can return the posts inside a conversation.
That is not the same as returning the conversation.
Paid Quota Does Not Remove Rate Limits
Recent search currently allows:

• 450 requests per 15 minutes per app

• 300 requests per 15 minutes per user

• 100 results per request
Those limits may be generous for one developer making a few searches.
They become much tighter when one application serves hundreds of customers.
If 45 users each open a large conversation requiring ten pages, that consumes all 450 app-level search requests for the 15-minute window.
Your account may still have plenty of paid quota available, but the application cannot perform another recent search until the limit resets.
This is how developers end up receiving 429 Too Many Requests while they still have credits.
The problem is not that 450 requests is always too low.
The problem is that a single user action can require dozens of requests, while the limit is shared across the entire application.
MCP Makes the API Callable, Not Plug-and-Play
X deserves credit for recognizing how developers build software today.
Its official MCP server, XMCP, exposes more than 200 API operations to compatible AI tools. An agent can search posts, inspect users, publish content, and perform other X actions.
But the MCP server still sits on top of the same API.
Developers need to run it locally, configure X credentials, and connect it to their AI client. Every tool call inherits the same pricing, pagination, result limits, and rate limits.
Ask an agent to inspect a discussion with 2,000 replies and it still needs to retrieve page after page.
Each reply remains a billable read.
Agents also tend to explore more than humans. They try alternative searches, inspect multiple conversations, look up authors, and follow related topics before producing an answer.
That is what makes them useful.
It also makes their API usage difficult to predict.
X has made its API callable by agents.
It has not made public Twitter data plug-and-play for them.
The Hardest Limit Is Trust
Even if X lowered every price tomorrow, another problem would remain.
Developers do not evaluate only today’s pricing. They consider whether today’s pricing, limits, and access model will still exist after they have spent six months building around them.
Twitter’s developer ecosystem has experienced years of major changes. Access models changed. Products were retired. Prices increased dramatically. Developers were forced to redesign or close applications.
The platform has now changed direction again.
Some of those decisions may have made sense for X as a business.
But developers remember them.
You cannot plan a sustainable product using only today’s API price. You need confidence that tomorrow’s rules will resemble the ones you integrate today.
X can reopen an API with a product update.
Rebuilding developer trust requires years of stability.
Where the Official API Still Makes Sense
The official X API remains the right choice when you need to:

• Publish or reply on behalf of users

• Access direct messages

• Manage likes, follows, or bookmarks

• Work with authenticated user data

• Build official account integrations
FetchLayer is not intended to replace those capabilities.
Public-data monitoring is a different problem.
Developers also need a straightforward way to search posts, retrieve user content, inspect discussions, and use Twitter data inside AI workflows — without building pagination, conversation reconstruction, rate-limit handling, sessions, proxies, and retry systems from scratch.
That is why we added Twitter to FetchLayer.
The Layer We Wanted as Developers
FetchLayer is built for retrieving and structuring public Twitter data.
There is no X developer application to create and no OAuth flow required for public search. Developers do not have to design their architecture around separate endpoint-specific windows such as 450 searches every 15 minutes.
A search looks like a normal API request:
const response = await fetch(
"https://fetchlayer.dev/api/twitter/search",
{
method: "POST",
headers: {
Authorization: "Bearer sk-your-api-key",
"Content-Type": "application/json"
},
body: JSON.stringify({
query: "developers complaining about API pricing"
})
}
);const data = await response.json();The goal is not to return a bag of replies and leave you to reconstruct the discussion.
The goal is to return useful, structured data directly.
FetchLayer also runs as a hosted MCP server:
{
"mcpServers": {
"fetchlayer": {
"url": "https://mcp.fetchlayer.dev",
"headers": {
"Authorization": "Bearer sk-your-api-key"
}
}
}
}That is the entire setup.
There is no repository to clone, no local process to maintain, and no separate X credentials to configure.
X’s MCP server makes its API available to an agent.
FetchLayer makes public Twitter data immediately usable by the agent.
Someone Still Has to Handle the Complexity
Reliable Twitter data still requires infrastructure.
Sessions must be maintained. Platform changes must be detected. Requests need to be routed correctly. Pagination, retries, extraction, and data structuring all have to work together.
FetchLayer handles that layer behind the API.
The complexity has not disappeared.
It has simply become our responsibility instead of yours.
Developers ultimately have three choices:

• Use X’s API and build around its pricing, limits, and policies.

• Build and maintain their own extraction infrastructure.

• Use a managed service that maintains it for them.
We built FetchLayer for the third option.
Opening the API Was Only the Beginning
X has made its API easier to access again.
That matters.
But developer ecosystems are not rebuilt by access alone. Developers need economics that still work when their products grow, tools designed around the data they actually want, and confidence that the rules will remain stable.
A search endpoint returning 100 posts is access.
A complete conversation your application can immediately analyze is a product.
For publishing, account management, and authenticated integrations, the official API remains the obvious choice.
For monitoring, research, and AI agents that need reliable, structured public Twitter data, that is the gap we built FetchLayer to fill.
X’s API is open again.
Now it has to prove that developers can safely build on it.
